description: Complete guide to circular linked lists: singly circular and doubly circular. Operations include insertion, deletion, traversal, and applications like round-robin scheduling.

sections.title: What is a Circular Linked List?
sections.definition: In a circular linked list, the last node points back to the first node (or head) instead of nullptr. Can be singly or doubly circular.
sections.analogy: A roundabout or a ring: you can keep going around forever.

sections.title: Singly Circular Linked List - Insertion at Beginning
sections.definition: Insert new node, then traverse to last node to update its next to new node, and set head to new node. O(n). Alternatively, maintain tail pointer for O(1).

sections.title: Insertion at End (with tail pointer)
sections.definition: Keep a tail pointer to last node for O(1) insertion at end. Update tail->next to new node, new node's next to head.

sections.title: Deletion in Circular Linked List
sections.definition: Delete by value: find node, adjust previous node's next to skip it. Handle head deletion specially. O(n).

sections.title: Traversing a Circular List
sections.definition: Use do-while loop until you come back to head. Be careful with empty list.

sections.title: Josephus Problem (Circular Elimination)
sections.definition: People standing in a circle, every k-th person is eliminated. Solved using circular linked list.

sections.title: Doubly Circular Linked List
sections.definition: Each node has prev and next. Head's prev points to tail, tail's next points to head. Allows bidirectional traversal.

sections.title: Applications of Circular Linked Lists
sections.definition: Round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, multiplayer games (turn-based), undo/redo functionality.
